title: Agent Auth: Cryptographic Identity for AI Agents
description: A guided tour of kanoniv-agent-auth, a cross-language cryptographic delegation framework that provides the missing authentication layer for MCP servers. Explore the Rust, TypeScript, and Python implementations, delegation chains, and framework integrations.

steps.title: The Big Picture
steps.description: Welcome to agent-auth — a cryptographic identity and delegation framework for AI agents. The project provides byte-identical implementations across Rust, TypeScript, and Python so that agents written in any language can prove authority to servers in any other language. The core modules handle identity (cryptographic keypairs and DIDs), signing, delegation chains with caveats, MCP proof injection, and provenance audit trails.

steps.title: Rust Core — The Source of Truth
steps.description: The canonical implementation lives in src/. Rust is the source of truth for cross-language compatibility. The identity module generates cryptographic keypairs and derives DIDs. The delegation module enforces caveat chains that control what actions agents can perform, when, and at what cost. The MCP module handles proof extraction and injection for tool calls.

steps.title: TypeScript Implementation
steps.description: The js/ directory contains a modern TypeScript implementation published to npm as @kanoniv/agent-auth. It mirrors the Rust API exactly — same modules for identity, signing, delegation, MCP, and provenance. The CLI provides command-line access to key generation and verification. Tests validate byte-identical output against shared fixtures.

steps.title: Python + PyO3 Bridge
steps.description: The Python implementation uses PyO3 to expose Rust cryptographic operations as a native Python extension. The src/lib.rs file contains the FFI bindings, while the Python package in kanoniv_agent_auth/ provides a clean Pythonic API. Maturin handles the build pipeline from Rust to installable Python wheel.

steps.title: Cross-Language Test Fixtures
steps.description: The fixtures/ directory is the linchpin of cross-language compatibility. These deterministic test vectors — generated from known secret keys — ensure that all three implementations produce byte-identical DIDs, content hashes, signatures, and delegation verification results. Every language's test suite validates against these shared fixtures.

steps.title: Framework Integrations
steps.description: Drop-in integrations for popular AI agent frameworks. The MCP SDK middleware (TypeScript and Python) adds delegation verification to any MCP server. Additional integrations cover CrewAI, LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK, and AutoGen — each wrapping the core auth primitives into framework-idiomatic patterns like decorators and middleware.

steps.title: Demos & Examples
steps.description: The demo/ directory showcases multi-agent orchestration scenarios including healthcare delegation, financial operations with cost tracking, and agent debate with competing authorities. The examples/ directory has runnable MCP server/client demos. Tutorials walk through LangGraph multi-agent handoff with budget constraints and revocation.

steps.title: The Complete Architecture
steps.description: Agent-auth delivers a single trust primitive — cryptographic delegation — across three languages with byte-identical guarantees. The Rust core is the source of truth. TypeScript and Python mirror it exactly. Shared fixtures enforce cross-language compatibility. Framework integrations make adoption a one-liner. Together, they give AI agents verifiable authority without implicit trust or shared API keys.
